item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here and is licensed under an NC "non-commercial" license. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be very useful to the foundation in general. Shame it hasn't been used in any stories I've seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that the 2nd paragraph was written poorly. It mentions that the user is turned invisble multiple times just explained/worded differently, as well as compaing it to an ocotopus. I'm aware that it's based off an ocotopus, but regardless invisible is invisible regardless to what you compare it to.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if HyaenaBoy or FortuneFavorsBold is making me facepalm harder here. Do I really need to get into why? I'll do it anyway.

No, you don't "really need to" get into why, because people are allowed to have their opinions about a piece and vote as they wish about that piece.

That said, keep in mind that just as you're fully entitled to your opinion, so is everyone else. You're not obligated to go after anyone you think is "wrong", nor should you be alienating people based on your beliefs about the site (i.e. "That's what people come to this site to see - if that's not what you're looking for, you shouldn't be here").

You can disagree with someone without launching into a long explanation of why you disagree with them. I'm frankly unsure of how you keep feeling the "need" to clash with other members in this manner. Calm down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if HyaenaBoy or FortuneFavorsBold is making me facepalm harder here. Do I really need to get into why? I'll do it anyway.

It's a weird thing. It makes no sense according to the laws of the universe as they are currently understood. Ergo, the Foundation must contain it. That's what people come to this site to see - if that's not what you're looking for, you shouldn't be here. The "thing what makes you fight good" gimmick - which this isn't, but even if it did camouflage the clothing, in which case it might arguably be - isn't something that must never be done ever, it's just more difficult to make one unique; I'd say making someone, for many intents and purposes, an octopus is pretty unique, no matter how useful it is in terms of practicality. Things don't have to be scary or even slightly dangerous to qualify for SCP. If someone was attacked for writing a scip that wasn't scary, either the way the scip was written strongly implied that it should have a negative side effect and without it the article felt unfinished, or the author was being attacked by people who don't understand what the Foundation is supposed to be about, in which case the attackers would be wrong. (Also, if anyone really hasn't seen a scip with benefits and no downsides, go look at SCP-500.)

So anyway, yeah…With that off my chest: thing what makes you an octopus. Very nice, I like it a lot. Hey, if the person can't be seen, do they maybe actually turn into an octopus, but no one is any the wiser for it? Even with the clothes, that could work - look up Octodad. Anyway, something that confused me is that the title of this scip is "clockwork octopus", but apart from having a nonfunctional timepiece, there's nothing about this octopus that is clockwork. Maybe use Smapti's comment and call it "In An Octopus's Garden" or something? The song's very fitting, I can't deny it, even though the connection my brain made was Octodad…I know not everyone even sees the titles of these things before reading them, so it's not really important by any means; it's just that I expected something totally different (and, I admit, much less interesting) than what the article turned out to be.

Also, TL333s's comment is why I love reading discussion pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool picture, thought-provoking effect, raises questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this one rated so high? This is a flat magical item, of practically no existential or narrative interest.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All awsomeness aside, i would love to be an octopus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, that's a leftover from before I edited it to make the subject naked. I'll fix that.
EDIT: I've posted this twice. Damn you, laggy internet connectioooon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, that's a leftover from before I edited it to make the subject naked. I'll fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why doesn't it work as a necklace? If you're not wearing clothing it should still be touching your skin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just goofy as hell and I love it.

Are We Underwater Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a clarification: Object class refers to how difficult an object is to contain, not how dangerous it could potentially be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't need any drawbacks. The naked thing is fine because that extends naturally from its properties, but the driving you CRAZY! is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I have no idea how I'd implement the octopus mind thing. It's cool but I'm at a loss of how to write a human being acting like an octopus in a realistic and not-silly sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe I completely forgot to add the nakedness bit. That was a part of it D: As for the octopus mindset thing, that's a really great idea and I'll see where I can put that in.
Good to know there are fellow bears on this site! I was worried I was alone in my bearity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. I think it's quirkily (it's a word! shut up!) anomalous without being over the top. As for negative side effects, I don't really think it needs any, but you could have something like prolonged exposure makes the user gradually think that s/he actually IS an octopus, even when not using the SCP. I do want to know how it affects clothing, however. I get that the person's skin becomes chromataphoric allowing them to change colors to match backgrounds, but this happens to the clothing too? Otherwise they'd have to be naked to get the full effects, which is kinda a negative side effect (or rather, a lack of positive side effect) in and of itself.

As an aside, it's good to see more Authorbears on the site. Welcome, my squamous grizzly cousin!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's mentioned that the camouflage is significantly better than that of a regular octopus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm perfectly fine with this thing being entirely benign. It's a well executed curiosity, and the site can always use more of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1182 causes the subject to camouflage perfectly with their surroundings. The sculpture causes an as of yet unexplained reaction on SCP-1182-1’s pigmentation, giving it the properties of an octopus in disguise.

If this works even when observed from more than one viewpoint it would require that pigmentation emit different colors of light in different directions, since when observed from different viewpoints a single point of the subject's surface could be covering up backgrounds of differing colors. While an anomalous item could certainly create anomalous pigments, what's described goes a lot beyond what an octopus does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like up until the third paragraph in the description… that's when it starts getting silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nooooooooo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Quirky, but interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are now hearing the song "Octopus' Garden" in your head whenever you think of SCP-1182.

That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Different people have different opinions but that doesn't mean every SCP needs to be deadly or have a noticeable drawback. In regards to this item I think the drawback is an individuals speed and that they aren't invisible, just really well hidden. Motion sensors and those heat cameras (I'm not a very smart man) would still detect the individual.

If they gained super octopus strength or something then maybe, but it's fine as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the underwater thing would cause a hindrance to getting around and understanding what's going on, but I see where you're coming from. I just don't know how many effects I can give the octopus without it being ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On my recent post, I got some flak because my SCP essentially didn't do enough damage. I kind of see what they mean when looking at 1182. It's, for all intensive purposes, an octopus-themed invisibility cloak that anyone can use with no side-effects. I don't remember reading one SCP that benefited the user and gave them extraordinary powers without some kind of drawback. I'm just suggesting adding another level to the SCP. It's a great picture; I don't want it to go to waste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a whole category of stuff the foundation keeps that doesn't hurt anyone. "Safe". The category this is a part of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation houses anomalous objects, deadly or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you approximately measure something in millimeters? Anyway, I would try to let back on the constant octopus implication. We know the object looks like an octopus, and it turns the wearer colors, like a cuttlefish. That's all the average human being needs to be able to make that connection. Some parts seems unnecessary and start making it feel like 'thing what makes fight good'. Is there a drawback to using this SCP, like an octopus jumping on you while you sleep and biting your dick off? If it can't hurt anybody, there really isn't a point to the Foundation keeping it under it's roof. Still voted up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's much better than what I have written. Not sure why I couldn't figure that out. Thank you, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about just "behaving as if submerged"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, I completely overlooked that outside observer part. Thanks for catching that.
Also, about the water physics part, I had no idea how to word that. I fiddled with it for a while before eventually giving up and settling on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I basically like this. A few nitpicks:

though to an outside observer they are simply walking slowly

How can an outside observer tell how they are walking if they are "effectively invisible"? Perhaps they are only totally invisible if standing still?

Other personnel in the room are seen normally, albeit with their hair and clothing responding to water physics.

I know what you mean by this, but the phrasing is really weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The correlation isn't between the invisibility and the water, it's related to the theme of an octopus. The subject takes on the camouflage properties of an octopus (although exaggerated) and sees themselves as being in the habitat of an octopus. Sometimes, even actually as an octopus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's neat, but I don't see any inherent correlation between perceiving yourself to be an aquatic being and being invisible. It's also pretty useful, all in all, which bothers some people (though I don't generally find that too disturbing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In what way is the underwater bit confusing? I'll try to iron it out if I know what's wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's well-written and I'm not downvoting, but it's not quite enough to grab me conceptually. SCP-408 has the camouflage ability, in a somewhat more creative way. The seeing-the-world-underwater thing is neat, but somewhat confusing. It would be a great Anomalous Object, but it's not much as an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and sweet, not too flashy. It doesn't wow me but I don't see any reason to downvote it either. +1
